Name: structural_prior_networks_for_crime_forecasting
Title: Structural Prior Networks: Co-Evolving Graph Structure and Dynamics for Spatial-Temporal Crime Forecasting
Short Hypothesis: Crime forecasting accuracy can be significantly improved by jointly modeling the evolution of spatial dependencies (e.g., between neighborhoods) alongside crime event dynamics—where the graph structure is not fixed or separately learned, but co-evolves with the temporal process through an interpretable latent mechanism derived from criminological principles.
Related Work: Existing crime forecasting models such as Risk Terrain Modeling (RTM) and self-exciting point processes (e.g., SEPPs) assume static spatial influences or predefined neighborhood graphs. Recent GNN-based spatio-temporal models (e.g., STGCN, ST-MGCN) have been applied to traffic and ride-hailing demand, but treat graph structure as either fixed or independently inferred. In contrast, our approach draws inspiration from Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDEs), recently used in traffic (STG-NCDE), to propose a continuous-time framework where the spatial graph and crime intensity co-evolve. Unlike prior work, we do not decouple structure and dynamics; instead, we introduce a 'structural prior network' that adaptively updates the connectivity based on latent crime regime shifts—such as diffusion, displacement, or hotspot stabilization—grounded in theoretical expectations from environmental criminology.
Abstract: Accurate spatial-temporal crime forecasting requires capturing not only when and where crimes occur, but how the spatial dependencies between regions change over time due to underlying criminological processes like diffusion or displacement. We propose Structural Prior Networks (SPNs), a novel framework that co-evolves the spatial dependency graph and crime intensity dynamics using Neural Controlled Differential Equations (NCDEs). SPNs treat the adjacency matrix as a latent state that evolves jointly with crime counts, guided by interpretable structural priors—such as distance decay, land-use similarity, and historical co-activation patterns—that modulate edge updates. This enables the model to discover time-varying spatial regimes (e.g., temporary contagion effects after major incidents). Evaluated on real-world crime datasets (e.g., Chicago PD calls-for-service), SPNs outperform static and dynamic GNN baselines in forecasting accuracy, particularly during regime shifts. Additionally, the learned graph trajectories offer interpretable insights into evolving crime patterns, bridging machine learning and criminological theory.
Experiments: 1. Dataset: Use publicly available crime data (e.g., Chicago Open Data Portal) aggregated at census tract level (N ≈ 800) into hourly bins over 5 years. Preprocess to extract counts of Part I crimes (theft, assault, burglary).
2. Baselines: Compare against (a) Historical Average, (b) SEPP with kernel smoothing, (c) Graph WaveNet, (d) DCRNN, (e) STG-NCDE (adapted to discrete counts via Poisson loss).

3. Model: Implement SPN with two coupled NCDEs: one governing crime intensity $x(t)$, another governing adjacency $A(t)$, where controls are shared via a prior network $f_\theta(\cdot)$ that computes edge update tendencies based on exogenous features (distance, land use, socioeconomic similarity).
4. Training: Optimize using Poisson negative log-likelihood with adjoint method; constrain A(t) to remain sparse and row-normalized.

5. Evaluation Metrics: MAE, RMSE, and CRPS (Continuous Ranked Probability Score) for count forecasts; link prediction AUC for recovered edges; qualitative analysis of A(t) trajectories during known events (e.g., protests, policy changes).
Risk Factors and Limitations: The model may overfit to specific city layouts or crime types; evaluation across multiple cities (e.g., Chicago, Los Angeles, Philadelphia) will mitigate this.
Interpretability of learned graph dynamics depends on proper regularization; we will use sparsity penalties and visualize top edge changes.
Computational cost of NCDEs could be high for large graphs; we will use low-rank approximations of A(t) and subsampled training.
Assumes that spatial dependencies are symmetric; future work can extend to directed graphs for asymmetric influence (e.g., downtown → suburb).

Name: dual_graph_crime_forecasting
Title: Semantic-Physical Dual Graph Networks for Crime Forecasting: Modeling Cross-Space Contagion via Functionally Similar Locations
Short Hypothesis: Crime events spread not only through geographic proximity but also across functionally similar yet spatially distant locations (e.g., commercial zones, transit hubs), and modeling this dual-mode contagion via a learnable semantic-physical graph architecture will significantly improve forecasting accuracy—especially for repeat and near-repeat victimization patterns.
Related Work: Existing approaches such as Risk Terrain Modeling (RTM) and self-exciting point processes (SEPPs) focus exclusively on physical proximity and static environmental risk factors. Recent GNN-based methods (e.g., STGCN, DCRNN) adopt fixed geographic graphs, while dynamic variants (e.g., STDN, STFGNN) infer time-varying spatial dependencies but still assume all influences are geographically mediated. In contrast, our proposal introduces a dual-graph framework—one physical (geographic) and one semantic (functional similarity)—with adaptive cross-graph message passing. While GSNet incorporates semantic features, it does not decouple or dynamically route information between semantic and physical spaces. Our model uniquely learns when and how crime risk propagates 'over function' rather than 'over distance,' grounded in routine activity theory and capable of capturing long-range crime diffusion missed by current models.
Abstract: Accurate crime forecasting requires understanding not only local diffusion but also non-local propagation to functionally similar environments—a mechanism supported by criminological theories of routine activity and crime pattern formation. We propose Semantic-Physical Dual Graph Networks (SP-DGN), a novel architecture that models crime dynamics over two concurrent graphs: a physical graph based on geographic adjacency and a semantic graph derived from land use, POI density, socioeconomic indicators, and mobility patterns. SP-DGN employs a gated cross-graph attention mechanism that adaptively routes temporal updates between nodes in both graphs, allowing the model to capture contagion effects across spatially distant but functionally similar regions (e.g., two downtown retail districts). The framework is trained end-to-end using historical crime counts and evaluated on real-world datasets (e.g., Chicago, Philadelphia). Results show significant improvements in forecasting accuracy—especially for theft and burglary—over single-graph and static-baseline models. Visualization of learned cross-graph attention weights reveals interpretable long-range crime propagation pathways, offering actionable insights for law enforcement and urban planning.
Experiments: 1. Dataset: Use crime data from Chicago Open Data Portal and Philadelphia PPD, aggregated at census tract level (N ≈ 800 and 1,200 respectively) into daily bins over 5 years. Focus on Part I crimes (burglary, motor vehicle theft, robbery).

2. Graph Construction: (a) Physical Graph: k-nearest neighbors based on geographic centroids. (b) Semantic Graph: learned pairwise similarity using Jaccard index on POI categories, normalized mobility flow, and census demographics (income, population density).

3. Baselines: Compare against (a) Historical Average, (b) SEPP with dual kernel (spatial + semantic), (c) Graph WaveNet (single physical graph), (d) ST-MGCN (multi-graph but no cross-graph gating), (e) GSNet.

4. Model: Implement SP-DGN with two parallel GNN stacks (GCN or GAT) operating on each graph, connected via a cross-graph attention module that computes message weights conditioned on temporal features (day-of-week, holiday, season).
5. Training: Optimize using Poisson negative log-likelihood; apply focal loss to address class imbalance from rare events.
6. Evaluation Metrics: MAE, RMSE, CRPS for count prediction; AUC-PR for high-risk zone classification; qualitative analysis of top cross-graph attention edges during known crime waves.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Semantic similarity may be city-specific; transferability across cities needs evaluation.
Requires access to auxiliary data (POIs, mobility); however, these are publicly available in most major U.S. cities.
Interpretation of cross-graph attention depends on proper regularization; we will use ablation studies and visualization.
Assumes symmetric influence; future work can extend to directed semantic flows (e.g., from high- to low-income commercial areas).

Name: intervention_aware_graph_rewiring_for_crime_forecasting
Title: Intervention-Aware Graph Rewiring Networks: Modeling Policy-Induced Structural Shifts in Spatial-Temporal Crime Dynamics
Short Hypothesis: Crime forecasting accuracy improves when the model explicitly accounts for known law enforcement or social interventions as structural breaks that reconfigure the latent spatial dependency graph—via interpretable edge pruning (suppression) or redirection (displacement)—rather than treating them as mere covariates or ignoring them entirely.
Related Work: Existing crime forecasting models (e.g., SEPPs, GNNs) treat interventions as additive covariates or ignore them. Recent dynamic graph models like STG-NCDE and MegaCRN learn endogenous graph evolution but do not condition on exogenous events. CausalGNN introduces causal mechanisms in epidemic forecasting but assumes fixed transmission laws. In contrast, our work draws from *interventional causal representation learning*, proposing that real-world interventions (e.g., hotspot policing) induce *discontinuous structural changes* in the crime network. We introduce a model that learns to *rewire* the spatial graph at intervention timestamps using a gated edge modulation mechanism, grounded in criminological theories of displacement and suppression. This is distinct from attention-based weighting: it performs actual topological edits, making the model both more interpretable and causally aware.
Abstract: Crime patterns are not only influenced by historical trends and spatial proximity but also radically reshaped by discrete interventions such as police crackdowns, community programs, or curfews. Current forecasting models fail to capture these abrupt structural shifts, treating interventions as passive inputs rather than active causes of graph reconfiguration. We propose Intervention-Aware Graph Rewiring Networks (IGRN), a novel architecture that models known interventions as triggers for latent structural changes in the spatial dependency graph. IGRN employs a two-stream GNN: one for continuous crime dynamics, another for intervention-conditioned graph modulation. At each time step, if an intervention occurs, a gating network predicts edge-specific updates—pruning links (modeling suppression) or forming new long-range connections (modeling displacement)—based on location features and intervention type. The rewired graph then informs future forecasts. Evaluated on Chicago and Los Angeles crime data with annotated interventions (e.g., gang injunctions, foot patrols), IGRN outperforms static and dynamic baselines in post-intervention forecasting accuracy. Learned rewiring patterns align with criminological expectations, offering interpretable insights into policy impacts.
Experiments: 1. Dataset: Use crime data from Chicago PD and LAPD (5 years, daily resolution) at census tract level. Collect intervention logs: e.g., 'Operation Safe Streets', 'gang abatement zones' from public reports and news archives. Encode interventions as binary indicators per region and time (N ≈ 800 nodes).
2. Baselines: Compare against (a) Historical Average, (b) SEPP with intervention covariates, (c) DCRNN, (d) STG-NCDE, (e) GSNet with intervention features.

3. Model: Implement IGRN with (a) a primary GNN (e.g., GCN-LSTM) for crime dynamics, and (b) a rewiring module: given intervention type and location, predict ΔA ∈ ℝ^{N×N} via a sparse gated network with L1 regularization. Apply ΔA additively to A(t), followed by row-wise softmax to maintain stochasticity.
4. Training: Optimize Poisson NLL; use teacher forcing for pre-intervention steps, then free-run post-intervention to test generalization.

5. Evaluation Metrics: MAE, RMSE, CRPS for 7-/14-/30-day forecasts; AUC for high-risk zone classification; qualitative analysis of learned edge deletions/additions post-intervention (e.g., did downtown theft displace to suburbs?).
Risk Factors and Limitations: Intervention data may be noisy or incomplete; we will validate with multiple sources and conduct sensitivity analysis.
Assumes interventions have immediate effects; future work can model delayed responses via temporal convolution.
Rewiring may overfit to city-specific policies; evaluation across cities mitigates this.
Computational cost increases with full-graph rewiring; we will use low-rank parameterization of ΔA (e.g., u·v^T).

Name: memory_segregated_graph_networks_for_crime_forecasting
Title: Memory-Segregated Graph Networks: Disentangling Repeat and Near-Repeat Victimization in Spatio-Temporal Crime Forecasting
Short Hypothesis: Crime forecasting accuracy improves when the model explicitly segregates and updates memory states for self-victimization (repeat) and spatially diffused victimization (near-repeat)—via dedicated, gated memory modules—allowing adaptive, interpretable modeling of heterogeneous triggering dynamics beyond fixed kernel assumptions in SEPPs or monolithic GNNs.
Related Work: Self-exciting point processes (SEPPs) and their neural variants (Neural Hawkes) model repeat and near-repeat effects using fixed or learned kernels but assume homogeneous diffusion. GNN-based models (e.g., STGCN, DCRNN) aggregate neighborhood information uniformly, failing to distinguish between self-triggering and neighbor-triggering events. While ST-MGCN uses multiple graphs, it does not separate memory pathways. Our proposal introduces a dual-path memory architecture: one 'self-memory' module tracks per-location risk decay after victimization, and a 'neighbor-memory' module captures spatially diffused risk via graph propagation. These are updated by a gating network conditioned on event type and temporal context, enabling the model to learn when crime spreads locally versus re-emerges at the same site. This explicit disentanglement is novel in both ML and criminology-informed forecasting.
Abstract: Repeat and near-repeat victimization are fundamental patterns in crime dynamics, yet most forecasting models treat them implicitly through monolithic spatial smoothing or fixed excitation kernels. We propose Memory-Segregated Graph Networks (MSGN), a novel architecture that explicitly disentangles self-victimization (repeat) from spatially diffused victimization (near-repeat) using segregated, learnable memory modules. MSGN maintains two parallel hidden states: (1) a self-memory, updated only when a node experiences a crime, modeling local risk persistence; and (2) a neighbor-memory, updated via graph convolution, modeling spatial diffusion. A temporal gating network controls memory interaction based on event features (e.g., crime type, time-of-day). The model is trained end-to-end using historical crime counts and evaluated on real-world datasets (Chicago, Philadelphia). Results show significant improvements in forecasting accuracy—especially for burglary and theft—over SEPPs, GNNs, and attention-based baselines. Visualization of memory activations reveals interpretable risk decay curves and diffusion ranges, aligning with criminological findings and offering actionable insights for targeted policing.
Experiments: 1. Dataset: Use Chicago PD and Philadelphia PPD crime data (5 years, daily resolution) at census tract level. Focus on Part I crimes with high repeat victimization rates (burglary, motor vehicle theft).
2. Baselines: Compare against (a) Historical Average, (b) SEPP with power-law kernel, (c) Neural Hawkes Process, (d) Graph WaveNet, (e) ST-MGCN, (f) GSNet.

3. Model: Implement MSGN with two GRU-like memory units: (a) Self-Memory: updated only on self-events via a write gate; (b) Neighbor-Memory: updated via GCN from neighbors. A read gate combines both for prediction. Gating functions depend on crime type and temporal features (hour, day-of-week).
4. Training: Optimize using Poisson negative log-likelihood; apply L1 regularization on neighbor-memory weights to encourage sparsity in diffusion.

5. Evaluation Metrics: MAE, RMSE, CRPS for count forecasts; AUC-PR for high-risk zone classification; analysis of learned memory decay rates and effective diffusion radius (via neighbor-memory weights).
Risk Factors and Limitations: Memory segregation may not generalize to crime types with low repeat victimization (e.g., homicide); we will focus evaluation on burglary and theft.
Assumes memory updates are instantaneous; future work can model delayed activation via ODEs.
Requires sufficient event density per node; sparse regions may benefit from hierarchical pooling.
Interpretability depends on proper regularization; we will use ablation studies to validate memory roles.

Name: spatiotemporal_phase_transitions_for_crime_forecasting
Title: Phase-Transition Aware Spatio-Temporal Networks: Modeling Regime Shifts in Crime Dynamics via Unsupervised Change Point Detection
Short Hypothesis: Urban crime dynamics undergo discrete phase transitions (e.g., from stable to epidemic-like spreading) that can be detected unsupervised and exploited to improve forecasting accuracy—by adaptively switching or reinitializing model states at transition points, rather than assuming smooth evolution or stationarity across time.
Related Work: Most spatio-temporal crime forecasting models assume continuous, stationary dynamics and are trained end-to-end without accounting for abrupt shifts in underlying patterns. While recent works like STDN and STG-NCDE model dynamic spatial graphs, they do so smoothly over time. In contrast, our approach is inspired by *regime-switching models* in econometrics and *epidemic phase detection* in public health, but applied to crime. Unlike CausalGNN, which assumes known causal mechanisms, we propose an unsupervised method to detect structural breaks in the spatio-temporal process itself. Our idea is also distinct from intervention-aware models (e.g., IGRN), as it does not require labeled interventions—making it more generalizable. Instead, we use a lightweight change point detector on latent graph signals to identify phase transitions, then trigger adaptive memory resets or model interpolation, a mechanism not explored in current GNN or SEPP literature.
Abstract: Crime dynamics often shift abruptly due to hidden socio-environmental changes—such as seasonal migration, policy rollouts, or gang activity cycles—that violate the stationarity assumptions of most forecasting models. We propose Phase-Transition Aware Spatio-Temporal Networks (PT-STN), a novel framework that detects unsupervised phase transitions in crime patterns and adapts its forecasting behavior accordingly. PT-STN combines a base GNN forecaster (e.g., Graph WaveNet) with a lightweight change point detection module that monitors temporal deviations in graph-level statistics—such as modularity, entropy of crime distribution, or spectral properties of the co-activation graph. When a significant shift is detected, the model either resets node memory states (to avoid carrying outdated risk priors) or switches to an alternate set of parameters learned on that regime type. The full system is trained using self-supervised pre-training for regime discovery and fine-tuned end-to-end. Evaluated on Chicago and Los Angeles crime data, PT-STN outperforms static and continuously adapted baselines, especially during periods of rapid change. The detected phases align with known external events (e.g., summer spikes, post-pandemic shifts), offering interpretable, policy-relevant insights without requiring labeled interventions.
Experiments: 1. Dataset: Use daily Part I crime counts from Chicago PD and LAPD (5 years) at census tract level. Construct time-evolving co-activation graphs where edges represent significant pairwise crime co-occurrence (via lagged correlation).
2. Baselines: Compare against (a) Historical Average, (b) SEPP, (c) Graph WaveNet, (d) DCRNN, (e) STG-NCDE, and (f) STG-NCDE with periodic memory reset (ablation).

3. Model: Implement PT-STN with (a) a Graph WaveNet backbone; (b) a change point detector using E-divisive algorithm on rolling windows of graph entropy and modularity; (c) a memory reset mechanism: when a change point is detected, reset GRU hidden states to zero or low-variance prior.

4. Training: Pre-train change point detector on synthetic shifts (simulated regime changes); train forecaster normally, but log performance before/after detected transitions. Use Poisson NLL for optimization.
5. Evaluation Metrics: MAE, RMSE, CRPS overall and within 7 days post-transition; number of detected change points vs. known events (precision/recall); ablation on reset vs. no-reset.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Change point detection may be sensitive to noise; we will use robust statistics (e.g., median-based filters) and validate across multiple graph signals.
Memory reset might discard useful long-term trends; we will test partial resets (e.g., only high-risk nodes) and compare to adaptive interpolation.
Assumes phase transitions are global; future work can extend to localized regime shifts using node-level detectors.
Requires sufficient history for reliable detection (~6 months); not suitable for very short time series.
